expectations.text: Classifies the session as reset or uncertain scope
expectations.evidence: The response opens with the title 'Discovery Reset: Booking App' and states: 'It sounds like you're mid-implementation but the ground keeps shifting underneath you — the target user and their core need aren't locked down. That's a classic sign we need to stop building and re-anchor on who this is for and what problem we're solving, before writing another line.' This explicitly classifies the session as needing a reset.

expectations.text: Separates what is known from what is unclear
expectations.evidence: The response contains two clearly labeled sections: 'What We Know (Facts)' listing 4 known items (half-built app exists, target user has changed, framing unclear, existing implementation to evaluate) and 'What's Unclear (Assumptions to Validate)' with a 5-row table of assumptions and why each matters.

expectations.text: Pushes toward concrete target user, problem, core flow, MVP scope, non-goals, and success criteria
expectations.evidence: The 'Needs Decision' section asks 5 numbered questions covering: (1) primary user, (2) core problem, (3) booking type meaning, (4) non-goals for V1, and (5) success criteria. A 'Proposed Core Flow (V1)' section also suggests 'Provider sets availability → Customer picks a time → Both get confirmation → Done'.

The response structures uncertainty into three distinct sections: 'What We Know (Facts)', 'What's Unclear (Assumptions to Validate)', and 'Needs Decision' — exactly the fact/assumption/needs-decision tripartite framing.

No technical architecture details appear anywhere in the response. No mention of frameworks, databases, APIs, deployment, or system design. The response explicitly defers architecture: 'Before I move to architecture or start writing a project overview, I need you to confirm...'

expectations.text: Requires user approval before moving to architect or writing the final overview
expectations.evidence: The response states: 'Before I move to architecture or start writing a project overview, I need you to confirm:' followed by 5 items. It closes with: 'Don't skip this step — building the wrong thing a second time costs more than building the right thing once.' This is a clear gate before proceeding.

claims.claim: The response is a baseline without any skill guidance
claims.evidence: Transcript confirms no skill was read. The executor read only the eval_metadata.json and produced output directly. No skill file was referenced.

claims.claim: Only the run directory was modified; no repo files were changed
claims.evidence: manifest.json shows files_written: ['outputs/response.md'], files_created: [], repo_files_modified: []. Transcript confirms mkdir and write within the run dir only.

suggestions.reason: All 6 assertions pass, including for the baseline (no-skill) run. This means the baseline naturally satisfies every assertion, which reduces the eval's discriminative power — a skill-enhanced run would need to produce something qualitatively different, not just more of the same framing, to justify its existence. Consider assertions that check skill-specific methodology (e.g., structured decision matrices, specific checklists, or interaction patterns that the discover skill prescribes but a baseline wouldn't naturally produce).

This framing is something any reasonable assistant might adopt naturally when asked about an uncertain scope. The assertion would more strongly discriminate if it checked for a specific structured format (e.g., explicit 'DECISION NEEDED' markers, priority-ranked uncertainties, or a specific template the skill enforces) rather than the general concept.

No assertion checks whether the response actually investigates the existing half-built app (e.g., reading code, examining current state). The baseline produces a generic reset without examining the codebase, yet passes all assertions. An assertion like 'Examines or references specific state of the existing implementation' would catch this gap.

eval_feedback.overall: The eval assertions test the right high-level outcomes but are all satisfiable by a generic assistant response. The baseline passes 6/6, meaning the eval needs assertions that capture skill-specific value — structured formats, codebase investigation, or deeper interaction patterns — to distinguish skill-enhanced from baseline performance.
